moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unusual but flexible move—the Van Geet Opening. By developing the knight before committing our central pawns, we keep maximum flexibility and avoid the heavily-analyzed main lines. This move controls d5 and e4, preparing to support our center while keeping our options open about whether to play d4 or e4 later. It's a solid choice if you enjoy creative, positional chess rather than memorizing long theoretical lines.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center, which is natural and strong. We now have the choice to contest d5 or work around it—the Van Geet's flexibility means we're not forced into any specific plan yet.

We support the center and prepare to develop our light-squared bishop along the long diagonal. This move is quieter than d4, which would lead to immediate central tension; instead, we're building a solid setup reminiscent of the Pirc Defense structures. Notice how d3 also prepares g3, allowing us to fianchetto our kingside bishop for long-term control of the center from afar.

Black develops a piece and pressures our e4 square, making a classical response. We're now in the Venezolana Variation territory, where Black plays direct, solid moves while we prepare our fianchetto setup.

We complete our fianchetto setup, preparing to place our bishop on g2 where it will exert long-term pressure on the d5 pawn and the long diagonal toward a1. This gives us a flexible, solid position—we can follow up with Bg2, then slowly build pressure or advance our center as opportunities arise. The Van Geet with g3 and Bg2 is less forcing than aggressive lines, but it gives us a comfortable, logical setup where we can outplay our opponent in the middlegame.
